result:
In the orange-washed glow of early autumn I find myself walking a little-known boardwalk that straddles the edge of an urban swamp. Cypress knees rise like ancient altar stones out of mirror-calm water, and somewhere to my left a heron-a statue with a heartbeat-explodes into slow flight. The air smells of bay leaf, leaf rot, diesel from the nearby interstate, and the indefinable mineral perfume of urban water doing its best impression of wilderness. I did not come for the scenery; I came for the permission this place offers. The permission to exist without explanation, to abandon biography almost exactly where the city ends and the wild begins, yet not far enough away to lose my cell signal. I toggle off airplane mode—the gesture feels ceremonial, like snuffing a candle in reverse—and then I toggle it back on again, deciding that outside time can wait. This, too, is a form of devotion.

I once read that writing is a migratory instinct—words traveling home by no single route, some drifting on thermals, others hitchhiking in the damp creases of strangers’ palms. Imagine the stories that ride the backs of barn swallows from Caracas to Chicago, tucked under iridescent feathers like contraband love notes. Those swallows are notorious border-jumpers, rarely questioned by the officials whose binoculars only look out, never in. Language behaves the same way: borderless, illiterate toward passports, allergic to typeset formalities. Whenever I worry that I have run out of things to say, I remember the barn swallows. They never fret about originality; they simply read the sky and adjust their wingbeats accordingly.

On the boardwalk a single sneaker—left foot, children’s size—lies with its tongue collapsed in an expression of weary surprise. A stranded mermaid slipper, mint green faded to the color of hospital corridors. Whoever she was, she was running—I can tell by the way the Velcro strap still curls forward, as if caught mid-motion. One can reconstruct entire cosmologies from what the world forgets: the location of the sprint, the nature of the limp, whether tears changed salinity levels in marsh water too small to register on NOAA charts. I once believed that to write about loss you needed tragedy close at hand, preferably with ambulance lights strobing magenta across the reader’s cornea. Now I think loss is the negative space between a small foot and its absent mate, the remaining shoe waiting years for a twin it will never meet. A tragedy compressed like bonsai, pruned daily by weather.

Yesterday my neighbor dragged her broken air conditioner to the curb. The unit stood waist-high,obsolete aluminum glacier-sweating Freon nostalgia. By evening street artists had tagged it with pastel hearts and the words “DO NOT LOVE THIS.” The imperative froze me in place. I read it as a memento mori aimed at exactly the sort of person who would discover anthropomorphic sorrow inside an appliance. Art has become that friend who refuses to let you wallow correctly, who redecorates your abyss in Pantone hues and stickers of ironic daisies. Yet the graffitied warning could be read another way: Do not love this because there is better to come, because attachment is a snug coffin you keep polishing from the inside. I left the AC where it sat, but paused long enough for the compressor to wheeze one final exhausted syllable—maybe “good,” maybe “bye,” maybe just a mechanical death rattle indifferent to rhyme schemes.

Walking again. Cicadas crank their delirious sewing machines overhead. The temperature is an argument between seasons, August and September tugging the thermostat sleeve like rival siblings. Where the railing’s green paint has peeled away, rust blossoms into continents. Between two planks someone has wedged a broken guitar pick stamped with the word TONK. I mouth the neologism aloud and it feels indecent, like crunching on peppermints made of questionable vowels. My phone buzzes: a headline insists that dopamine fasting is the new biohacking frontier. I imagine millions unplugging simultaneously, raising silence the way surfers await set waves. Meanwhile dopamine idles in the synaptic foyer wondering if the party was canceled, if the guests will arrive dressed as absence. Perhaps that is what the swamp air is offering—an ancient biochemistry hour during which the ledger forgets its lines.

Farther along, the boardwalk lists above deeper water. Below, sunfish weave through submerged grocery carts like teenagers on joyrides. Urban planners must hold late-night brainstorming sessions titled “How Do We Get Shopping Carts Knee-Deep Before Security Knows?” Is this entropy’s love language: the slow colonization of planned systems by unlicensed gardens? I once lost a novel manuscript to a laptop drowned in fountain water. After the grief fog settled, I pictured the Word files drifting apart like seed pods, cultivating vocabulary coral under oxidized pennies. The novel is perhaps growing there still: adjectives sprouting polyps, verbs glinting like paranoid minnows. Maybe the swamp is teaming with such derelict chronicles, polycultural substrate where literature goes to multicellular reincarnation.

I come upon a bench someone has dedicated “To whoever needs to breathe.” An invocation cast in bronze, impervious to both graffiti and good sense. Already the bench hosts a in iterative layers: skateboarder with neon shoelaces, elder guy in hospital bracelet apparently AWOL from dialysis, mother juggling toddler and bilingual audiobook, me jostling for elbow room with my complicated gladness. At first we sit in mutual suspicion, each thinking the others are napping<|reserved_token_163691|>

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
